Defendants And Crosscomplainants Laura Ozyilmaz And Sayat Ozyilmaz?S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Consolidate Actions [C.R.C. 3.350; C.C.P. ? 1048(A)]
Off Calendar Jun-23-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: Defendants And Cross-Complainants Laura Ozyilmaz And Sayat Ozyilmaz's Motion To Consolidate Actions [C.R.C. 3.350; C.C.P. Sec. 1048(A)].

No appearances made by the parties. The Court adopts its tentative ruling:

Defendants and cross-complainants Ozyilmazs' motion to consolidate is granted. Litz v. Ozyilmaz, CGC-19-580935 and FFGRG v. Ozyilmaz, CGC-19-581427 are consolidated for all purposes. The court designates Litz v. Ozyilmaz, CGC-19-580935 as the lead case and all future filings shall be made under Litz v. Ozyilmaz, CGC-19-580935.

When ruling on a motion to consolidate the court considers the common issues of law and fact, timeliness, complexity of the cases, and prejudice to the parties. Here, both actions involve the same transaction, i.e., the Noosh restaurant venture. The same essential parties and instruments are present in each action and failing to consolidate the matters will lead to a waste of party and court resources and possibly inconsistent rulings. The motion is timely. Discovery is in its fledgling state and neither action has a trial date. Consolidation will not unduly confuse the trier of fact and will benefit-not prejudice-all of the parties, including plaintiff Litz.

Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order.

Defendant And Cross-Complainant Laura Ozyilmaz And Sayat Ozyilmaz?S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Mediationarbitration And To Stay Claims Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Lucinda L. Storm Request For Judicial Notice
Off Calendar Jun-23-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: Defendant And Cross-Complainant Laura Ozyilmaz And Sayat Ozyilmaz's Motion To Compel Mediation/Arbitration And To Stay Claims.

No appearances made by the parties. The Court adopts its tentative ruling:

Defendants and cross-complainants Ozyilmazs' motion to compel "mediation/arbitration" is denied.

Defendants' motion to compel mediation is denied. There is no recognized authority allowing such relief as mediation is voluntary and a party may withdraw at any time. (See Gaines v. Fidelity National Title Ins. Co. (2016) 62 Cal.4th 1081, 1103; Jeld-Wen, Inc. v. Superior Court (2007) 146 Cal.App.4th 536, 541.)

Defendants' motion to compel arbitration is denied. Defendants bear the burden of proving the existence of a valid arbitration agreement by a preponderance of the evidence. (Pinnacle Museum Tower Assn. v, Pinnacle Market Development (US), LLC (2012) 55 Cal.4th 223, 236). In this case, defendants fail to demonstrate the existence of an agreement to arbitrate. Section 14 of the adduced Memorandum of Understanding ("MOU") provides the following:

If the parties are unable to resolve a material matter, they agree to submit to a full day of mediation or such lesser time as necessary to resolve the matter, to be administered by the American Arbitration Association under its Commercial Mediation Rules, within five (5) business days following any party's request therefor. (Storm Dec., Ex. A.)

The MOU says nothing about arbitration. Recognizing this deficiency, defendants pivot to the arbitration agreement at section 25.13 of the unexecuted Shareholders' Agreement. Mr. Litz is not bound to arbitrate under the Shareholders' Agreement because he never agreed to its terms. "Because arbitration is a contractual matter, a party who has not agreed to arbitrate a controversy cannot be compelled to do so." (Harris v. Tap Worldwide, LLC (2016) 248 Cal.App.4th 373, 380.) The fact that Mr. Litz and defendants are shareholders in FFG Restaurant Group, Inc. is irrelevant. They became shareholders before the existence of the MOU and proposed Shareholders' Agreement. There is no evidence that they subsequently agreed to arbitrate their disputes.

Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order.
